commentary: Excited to share that I've joined Ridgeline Systems as a software engineer on the backend team. Building cloud infrastructure in Go from day one. Grateful to Marcus Chen and the team for the warm welcome. Let's build.
First month on a production Go codebase taught me more about error handling than four years of coursework. Wrapping errors with context instead of swallowing them changes how you debug at 2am.
We're hiring across our platform and backend teams in Seattle. If you love Go, Kubernetes, and building reliable cloud infrastructure, we'd love to talk. Links in comments.
Shipped my first major service refactor this week. Cutting our p95 latency in half came down to one thing: batching database calls we were doing in a loop. Profile before you optimize.
New post on my blog: a plain-language walkthrough of how I set up a homelab Kubernetes cluster for side projects. No magic, just YAML and patience. Link in comments.
Go 1.24 is here. Generic type aliases, improved tooling, and runtime performance gains. Read the release notes and try it on your projects today.
Reliability tip from the billing service trenches: make your retries idempotent before you make them aggressive. A retry storm on a non-idempotent endpoint is how you double-charge people.

Spent the weekend adding full-text search to Ridgeline, my self-hosted read-it-later side project. PostgreSQL's tsvector did most of the heavy lifting. Sometimes you don't need a separate search engine.
KubeCon + CloudNativeCon registration is open. Join thousands of engineers shaping the future of cloud native infrastructure. Early bird pricing ends soon.
Migrated our Terraform state to a remote backend with locking this sprint. No more 'who has the state file' Slack messages. If you're still running local state on a team, fix that first.

Co-organizing Cascade Code Collective has been one of the best parts of moving to Seattle. Our project night this month had folks demoing everything from a Go CLI to a homegrown synth. Come build with us.
Accessibility is not a feature you bolt on at the end. Sharing the audit checklist my team uses every sprint. Ship interfaces everyone can use.
Hot take that shouldn't be hot: write the boring code. The clever one-liner you're proud of today is the line your teammate (or future you) will misread in six months. Clarity beats cleverness.
We're hiring a mid-level backend engineer for my team. Go, distributed systems, and a culture that takes on-call seriously without burning people out. Remote-friendly across US time zones. DM me.
Dashboards are only as good as the questions they answer. Our new guide on building SLO-driven dashboards is live on the blog. Stop staring at CPU graphs and start measuring what users feel.
Reliability tip I learned the hard way during on-call: gate every rollout on p95 latency, not averages. Averages hide the pain a slice of your users feel on every request.
Spent a week building a proper incident runbook template. The best on-call experience is the one where the tired person at 3am doesn't have to think, just follow steps. Sharing ours in comments.

One year at Ridgeline Systems today. Went from nervously opening my first pull request to owning a service in production. Huge thanks to my manager Marcus Chen and teammates for the mentorship. On to year two.
Rewrote a hot path from an ORM to hand-written SQL and the query dropped from 400ms to 12ms. ORMs are great until they aren't. Know what your framework is generating under the hood.
Lambda now supports longer execution timeouts and improved cold start performance for several runtimes. Read the announcement and see what it means for your serverless workloads.
New blog post: how I cut Ridgeline's Docker image from 800MB to 40MB with multi-stage builds and a distroless base. Smaller images mean faster deploys and a smaller attack surface. Link in comments.

Pairing remains the most underrated debugging tool. Spent two hours stuck on a race condition, then explained it out loud to Jason on a call and we found it in five minutes. Rubber-ducking, but the duck talks back.
